metadata.title: Chapter 1: The Foundations
metadata.subtitle: 1.1 The Alphabet
metadata.scene: These are the sounds that make the Dutch alphabet unique to English ears. Listen carefully to the vowels.

content: The Dutch alphabet consists of 26 letters. Click each letter to hear its standard pronunciation.

metadata.title: Chapter 1: The Foundations
metadata.subtitle: 1.2 Distinct Consonants
metadata.scene: These are the consonant sounds that Dutch uses differently from English. Some have no direct equivalent at all.

content: Most consonants sound similar to English. However, a few have very distinct pronunciations. Note that P, T, and K are spoken without a puff of air.

metadata.title: Chapter 1: The Foundations
metadata.subtitle: 1.3 Short and Long Vowels
metadata.scene: The length of a vowel completely changes the meaning of a word in Dutch. Listen to the difference between short and long sounds.

content: Dutch vowels can be short or long. The spelling changes to indicate the length of the sound.

assets.pronunciation: nurs (ur as in 'fur', with rounded lips)

metadata.title: Chapter 1: The Foundations
metadata.subtitle: 1.4 Diphthongs
metadata.scene: Diphthongs are tricky combinations where your mouth shifts shape halfway through the sound.

content: Diphthongs are vowel combinations that glide from one sound to another. Click each to hear how the sounds blend together.

metadata.title: Chapter 1: The Foundations
metadata.subtitle: 1.5 Counting to 100
metadata.scene: Time to master the numbers. Pay close attention to the inversion rule after you pass twenty.

content: Let us start with the base numbers. Once you pass 20, Dutch uses an inverted counting rule where the unit is said before the ten. For example, 21 is "eenentwintig", which translates to "one-and-twenty".

metadata.title: Chapter 1: The Foundations
metadata.subtitle: 1.6 Order, Sequence, and Colours
metadata.scene: Here we introduce ordinal numbers for sequencing, followed by basic colours to give you your first descriptive words.

content: Ordinal numbers are essential for making lists and dates. Alongside these, we will introduce basic colours to give you your first descriptive vocabulary words.

metadata.title: Chapter 1: The Foundations
metadata.subtitle: 1.7 Greetings & Quick Responses
metadata.scene: These are your first Dutch words in the real world. Greetings open every conversation, so learn them until they feel automatic.

content: Let us learn some basic greetings and icebreakers. These simple phrases will help you feel conversational right from the start.

metadata.title: Chapter 2: Building Sentences
metadata.subtitle: 2.1 Who is doing it? (Subject Pronouns)
metadata.scene: Learn the core subject pronouns. Pay attention to the stressed and casual forms, as native speakers use the casual forms constantly in everyday speech.

content: Notice the cultural difference between the formal 'u' (for strangers or elders) and the informal 'jij/je'. In everyday speech, Dutch people often use reduced forms like 'k, ie, and 't.

metadata.title: Chapter 2: Building Sentences
metadata.subtitle: 2.2 The Core Verbs (Zijn and Hebben)
metadata.scene: Here are the present tense forms of the two most important irregular verbs in Dutch: zijn (to be) and hebben (to have).

content: The verb indicates the action and adapts to the subject. Most full verbs end in -en.

metadata.title: Chapter 2: Building Sentences
metadata.subtitle: 2.3 Nouns & Articles (de, het, een)
metadata.scene: Dutch has two definite articles for 'the': 'de' and 'het'. Diminutives always take 'het'.

content: 'De' is used for common nouns and 'het' for neuter nouns. The word 'een' means 'a' or 'an'. Remember that compound words always take the gender of the final word.

metadata.title: Chapter 2: Building Sentences
metadata.subtitle: 2.4 Plurals and Spelling Quirks
metadata.scene: To make nouns plural, usually add -en or -s. The golden rule is that all plural nouns take the article 'de', regardless of their singular gender.

content: Watch out for spelling changes to keep vowel sounds short or long. A small group of nouns takes an -eren ending instead.

metadata.title: Chapter 2: Building Sentences
metadata.subtitle: 2.5 Pointing Things Out (Demonstratives)
metadata.scene: Use 'deze' and 'die' for de-words and all plurals. Use 'dit' and 'dat' for singular het-words. Memory trick: de, deze, and die all start with the letter 'd'.

content: Use these words to point out specific objects, meaning "this" or "that".

metadata.title: Chapter 2: Building Sentences
metadata.subtitle: 2.6 Describing Things (Adjective Endings)
metadata.scene: Adjectives before a noun usually get an -e ending. The big exception is an indefinite singular het-word.

content: Adjectives placed after the verb do not change at all. Remember spelling rules apply when adding the -e ending.

metadata.title: Chapter 2: Building Sentences
metadata.subtitle: 2.7 Word Order and Asking Questions
metadata.scene: The standard sentence order is Subject-Verb-Rest. If you start a sentence with time or place, swap the verb and subject (this is called inversion).

content: To ask a yes/no question, simply put the verb first. Here are examples of inversion, followed by the essential question words.

assets.dutch: Morgen gaat mijn partner naar Rotterdam
assets.english: Tomorrow my partner goes to Rotterdam
assets.tts: Morgen gaat mijn partner naar Rotterdam
assets.pronunciation: mor-khun khaht mayn pahrt-nur nahr rot-tur-dahm

metadata.title: Chapter 3: Time, Dates, and Gathering Information
metadata.subtitle: 3.1 The Calendar
metadata.scene: Let us learn the days, seasons, and ordinal numbers needed for dates. Remember that Dutch days and months are not capitalised.

content: We have broken the calendar down into days, seasons, sequencing numbers, and relative time words to help you navigate your schedule.

metadata.title: Chapter 3: Time, Dates, and Gathering Information
metadata.subtitle: 3.2 Klokkijken (Telling Time)
metadata.scene: The Dutch analogue clock looks forward to the next hour starting from the half-hour mark. 2:30 is 'half drie' (halfway to three). Minutes revolve around this half-hour mark.

content: The Dutch analogue clock looks forward to the next hour starting from the half-hour mark. 2:30 is 'half drie' (halfway to three). Minutes revolve around this half-hour mark.

metadata.title: Chapter 3: Time, Dates, and Gathering Information
metadata.subtitle: 3.3 Asking Specific Questions
metadata.scene: You already know the basic question words. Now let us look at how to ask for quantities and specific choices using inversion to form full, practical sentences.

content: You already know the basic question words. Now let us look at how to ask for quantities and specific choices using inversion to form full, practical sentences.

assets.english: What kind of car is that?

metadata.title: Chapter 3: Time, Dates, and Gathering Information
metadata.subtitle: 3.4 Conjugating Regular Verbs
metadata.scene: To find the verb stem, remove '-en' from the infinitive. 'Ik' uses the stem. 'Jij/Hij/Zij/Het' use stem + t. Plurals use the full infinitive.

content: To find the verb stem, remove '-en' from the infinitive. 'Ik' uses the stem. 'Jij/Hij/Zij/Het' use stem + t. Plurals use the full infinitive.

metadata.title: Chapter 3: Time, Dates, and Gathering Information
metadata.subtitle: 3.5 Weather and Small Talk
metadata.scene: The Dutch love talking about the weather. Here are some essential phrases to help you break the ice and describe the climate.

content: The Dutch love talking about the weather. Here are some essential phrases to help you break the ice and describe the climate.

assets.dutch: Het is 30 graden in de schaduw
assets.english: It is 30 degrees in the shade
assets.tts: Het is dertig graden in de schaduw
assets.pronunciation: het is dehr-tikh khrah-dun in duh skhah-duu-oo

metadata.title: Chapter 4: Navigating the World
metadata.subtitle: 4.1 Negation Masterclass (Niet vs. Geen)
metadata.scene: Use 'geen' to negate indefinite nouns, and 'niet' to negate verbs, adjectives, and definite nouns.

content: Use 'geen' to negate indefinite nouns (nouns preceded by 'a/an' or plurals with no article). Use 'niet' to negate verbs, adjectives, and definite nouns.

assets.english: I do not have a car
I do not see the man

metadata.title: Chapter 4: Navigating the World
metadata.subtitle: 4.2 Getting Around and Directions
metadata.scene: Learn basic navigation, key landmarks, and how to sequence directions using 'eerst' (first), 'dan' (then), and 'daarna' (after that).

content: Learn basic navigation, key landmarks, and how to sequence directions using 'eerst' (first), 'dan' (then), and 'daarna' (after that).

metadata.title: Chapter 4: Navigating the World
metadata.subtitle: 4.3 Receiving the Action (Object Pronouns)
metadata.scene: These pronouns receive the action. Pay attention to the stressed and casual forms, as native speakers swallow the casual forms in normal speech.

content: These pronouns receive the action. In daily speech, the Dutch frequently use the 'unstressed' (swallowed) versions unless they are highlighting a specific person.

assets.english: He sees her (haar = stressed, d'r = casual)

assets.dutch: Ik geef het aan jou / je
assets.english: I give it to you (jou = stressed, je = casual)
assets.tts: Ik geef het aan jou je
assets.pronunciation: ik khayf het ahn yow / yuh

assets.english: Give it to me (mij = stressed, me = casual)
I call them (hen = formal, ze = casual)

metadata.title: Chapter 4: Navigating the World
metadata.subtitle: 4.4 Giving Commands and Softeners
metadata.scene: To give a command, simply use the stem of the verb. Because a blunt command can sound rude, the Dutch soften them using conversational fillers.

content: To give a command, simply use the stem of the verb. Because a blunt command can sound rude, the Dutch soften them using conversational fillers like 'even', 'maar', or 'nou'.

metadata.title: Chapter 4: Navigating the World
metadata.subtitle: 4.5 Hitting the Road (Transport)
metadata.scene: When taking a vehicle, use the preposition 'met' (with), except for bicycles, where you go 'op de fiets' (on the bike).

content: The verb 'gaan' (to go) is highly irregular. When taking a vehicle, use the preposition 'met' (with), except for bicycles, where you go 'op de fiets' (on the bike).

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.1 The Selfish Learner (Family & Others)
metadata.scene: Why did we wait until now to learn about other people? Because early language learning is inherently selfish. You must first learn to navigate the dark alone. Now, let us bring others into the context.

content: Why did we wait until now to learn about other people? Because early language learning is inherently selfish. You must first learn to navigate the dark alone. Now, let us bring others into the context.

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.2 Hobbies and the Hours Between
metadata.scene: What do you do when you are not speaking to strangers in the dark? Let us apply the present tense conjugations to some common hobbies.

content: To say you "like to" do something, add the word 'graag'. Let us apply the present tense conjugations to some common hobbies.

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.3 The Daylight Hours (Everyday Transactions)
metadata.scene: Before we return to the dark, we must survive the day. Here are the mundane, practical phrases you need for everyday transactions.

content: Here are the mundane, practical phrases you need for everyday transactions, work, and navigating public spaces.

assets.tts: Ik werk van negen tot vijf
assets.pronunciation: ik vehrk fahn nay-khun tot fayf

assets.english: May I have the bill, please?
I am looking for the supermarket

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.4 Preferences and the 'Why'
metadata.scene: A conversation requires depth. You need to express what you like and, more importantly, why.

content: A conversation requires depth. You need to express what you like and, more importantly, why. We use 'ik hou van' (I love) or 'ik vind ... leuk' (I like), paired with the conjunction 'want' (because).

assets.dutch: Ik fiets, want ik hou van de regen
assets.english: I cycle, because I love the rain
assets.tts: Ik fiets want ik hou van de regen
assets.pronunciation: ik feets vahnt ik how fahn duh ray-khun

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.5 Turning the Mirror (Asking Questions)
metadata.scene: Do not just talk about yourself; ask the Apparitions questions to keep the conversation flowing.

content: Do not just talk about yourself; ask the Apparitions questions. Remember your inversion rules (verb before subject) to keep the dialogue flowing naturally.

assets.english: What do you like to do?
What kind of work do you do?

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.6 Confessions in the Dark (Survival)
metadata.scene: When the illusion starts to slip and you cannot find the words, you must know how to confess your limitations.

content: Use these phrases to pause the conversation and gather your thoughts.

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.7 Initiating the Illusion (Roleplay)
metadata.scene: You are at a night cafe in Amsterdam. A stranger sits nearby.

content: The Apparitions are waiting. Here are the specific phrases you need to initiate dialogue with the strangers at the bus stop, the barista in the night cafe, or to summon a story from the shadows.

assets.english: Good evening. Is it cold tonight?
assets.speaker: you

assets.dutch: Ja, de bus is te laat.
assets.tts: Ja de bus is te laat
assets.pronunciation: yah duh bus is tuh laht
assets.speaker: them

assets.dutch: Hallo. Ik kom binnen uit de regen.
assets.english: Hello. I am coming in out of the rain.
assets.tts: Hallo Ik kom binnen uit de regen
assets.pronunciation: hah-low ik kom bin-nun owt duh ray-khun
assets.speaker: you

assets.english: What do you want to drink?
assets.speaker: them

metadata.title: Chapter 5: Conjuring Fluency
metadata.subtitle: 5.8 The Holy Grail (Synthesising the Night)
metadata.scene: The night is ending. Use everything you have learned to navigate your way home.

content: This is where all the fragments align. We are combining time, hobbies, preferences, negation, and questions into full, practical statements ready for the real world. Reflect on these before you speak.

assets.dutch: Ik drink geen koffie, want het is laat. En jij?
assets.english: I do not drink coffee, because it is late. And you?
assets.tts: Ik drink geen koffie want het is laat En jij
assets.pronunciation: ik drink khayn kof-fee vahnt het is laht en yay

assets.dutch: Morgen fiets ik naar de stad, want ik hou van de regen.
assets.english: Tomorrow I will cycle to the city, because I love the rain.
assets.tts: Morgen fiets ik naar de stad want ik hou van de regen
assets.pronunciation: mor-khun feets ik nahr duh staht vahnt ik how fahn duh ray-khun

assets.dutch: Waar is het station? Ik begrijp het niet.
assets.english: Where is the station? I do not understand it.
assets.tts: Waar is het station Ik begrijp het niet
assets.pronunciation: vahr is het stah-shon ik buh-khrayp het neet

assets.dutch: Ik lees een boek, want ik heb morgen geen tijd.
assets.english: I am reading a book, because I have no time tomorrow.
assets.tts: Ik lees een boek want ik heb morgen geen tijd
assets.pronunciation: ik lays un book vahnt ik hep mor-khun khayn tayt

assets.dutch: Wat een rotweer! Ik ga naar huis.
assets.english: What terrible weather! I am going home.
assets.tts: Wat een rotweer Ik ga naar huis
assets.pronunciation: vaht un rot-vayr ik khah nahr how-ees

assets.dutch: Ik wil graag afrekenen, want ik moet naar werk.
assets.english: I would like to pay, because I must go to work.
assets.tts: Ik wil graag afrekenen want ik moet naar werk
assets.pronunciation: ik vil khrahkh ahf-ray-kuh-nun vahnt ik moot nahr vehrk

assets.dutch: Kunt u mij helpen? Ik zoek de trein.
assets.english: Can you help me? I am looking for the train.
assets.tts: Kunt u mij helpen Ik zoek de trein
assets.pronunciation: kunt uu may hel-pun ik zook duh trayn
